original_hypothesis.title: External-call surface expansion: Ln.calculateQ1/Q2/Q3 are public and may be abused for gas-griefing within aggregators
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Ln exposes calculateQ1, calculateQ2, and calculateQ3 as public pure functions with large branching/assembly arithmetic. While they do not change state, protocols that integrate Ln as an external contract/library wrapper (e.g., via delegatecall or a predeploy math contract) might inadvertently expose these as callable endpoints and include them in multicall/aggregator workflows. Attackers could spam calls to increase node load or cause out-of-gas failures in batched calls that include these functions.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is operationally relevant when these libraries are deployed as callable contracts (common for shared math libraries on some stacks) or wrapped behind public endpoints; DoS via gas grief is realistic at the infrastructure/application layer even without on-chain state changes.
deployment_assumptions: Project deploys Ln as a standalone callable contract or exposes it through a router/multicall that users can invoke
Integrator includes these calls in bundled operations where a revert/OG affects stateful steps
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: No deployment scripts provided showing whether Ln is deployed as a callable contract; requires Phase D deployment review.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function calculateQ2(uint256 mantissa) public pure returns (...) { ... large assembly blocks ... } (public visibility)

preconditions: Ln is deployed/exposed as an externally callable contract endpoint (not just linked library code)
There exists an operational workflow where third-party calls to these endpoints consume shared gas budget or cause batch failures
Attackers can economically justify spamming calls (low cost / high impact on service)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Public pure functions with heavy branching/assembly can be used as cheap grief targets when exposed behind shared-call infrastructure.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Continuous, if endpoint is public and monitored services are sensitive

operational_questions: Is Ln deployed as a contract on-chain or only linked as a Solidity library?
Are there routers/multicalls that forward arbitrary calls to Ln?
Do any off-chain services (keepers/relayers) batch calls including Ln functions and fail if one call consumes too much gas?
validation_requirements: Review deployment artifacts for Ln bytecode deployment
Benchmark gas usage for calculateQ1/Q2/Q3 worst-case branches and assess batch failure risk

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Confirm Ln is callable externally (deployed address).
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Craft inputs that take the most expensive code paths (largest branch sequences).
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Spam calls or embed them in batch calls to cause consistent OOG in shared workflows.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Exploit operational disruption (failed batch executions, increased infra costs).

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Service degradation / batch execution failures; increased operational costs
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low to Medium (rate limiting / endpoint isolation)
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Relayer/keeper/multicall workflows if they include these calls

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: High frequency calls to Ln contract addresses with varying mantissa values
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Spikes in RPC CPU usage correlated with these selectors

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
